name: Speedrun card-check: LLM-generated flashcards v1
note: Flashcards generated by tools/speedrun_card_check.py STRICTLY from the single source excerpt below. Committed so the check is reproducible offline from the cache. Each card carries the source sentence it was grounded in ('cite').

source.title: Cellular respiration
source.attribution: Wikipedia contributors, "Cellular respiration," Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia (revision 1355541234, retrieved 2026-07-02). Brief excerpt reused under CC BY-SA 4.0.
source.text:
Cellular respiration is the process of oxidizing biological fuels using an inorganic electron acceptor, such as oxygen, to drive production of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), which stores chemical energy in a biologically accessible form. Cellular respiration may be described as a set of metabolic reactions and processes that take place in the cells to transfer chemical energy from nutrients to ATP, with the flow of electrons to an electron acceptor, and then release waste products. If the electron acceptor is oxygen, the process is more specifically known as aerobic cellular respiration. If the electron acceptor is a molecule other than oxygen, this is anaerobic cellular respiration – not to be confused with fermentation, which is also an anaerobic process, but it is not respiration, as no external electron acceptor is involved. The reactions involved in respiration are catabolic reactions, which break large molecules into smaller ones, producing ATP.

Nutrients that are commonly used by animal and plant cells in respiration include sugar, amino acids and fatty acids, and the most common oxidizing agent is molecular oxygen (O2).

Biology textbooks often state that 38 ATP molecules can be made per oxidized glucose molecule during cellular respiration (2 from glycolysis, 2 from the Krebs cycle, and about 34 from the electron transport system). However, this maximum yield is never quite reached because of losses due to leaky membranes as well as the cost of moving pyruvate and ADP into the mitochondrial matrix, and current estimates range around 29 to 30 ATP per glucose.

Aerobic metabolism is up to 15 times more efficient than anaerobic metabolism (which yields 2 molecules of ATP per 1 molecule of glucose).

The post-glycolytic reactions take place in the mitochondria in eukaryotic cells, and in the cytoplasm in prokaryotic cells.

Glycolysis is a metabolic pathway that takes place in the cytosol of cells in all living organisms. Glycolysis can be literally translated as "sugar splitting", and occurs regardless of oxygen's presence or absence. The process converts one molecule of glucose into two molecules of pyruvate (pyruvic acid), generating energy in the form of two net molecules of ATP. Four molecules of ATP per glucose are actually produced, but two are consumed as part of the preparatory phase.

Glucose + 2 NAD+ + 2 Pi + 2 ADP → 2 pyruvate + 2 NADH + 2 ATP + 2 H+ + 2 H2O + energy

An additional ATP is used to phosphorylate fructose 6-phosphate into fructose 1,6-bisphosphate by the help of phosphofructokinase.

Pyruvate is oxidized to acetyl-CoA and CO2 by the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC). The PDC contains multiple copies of three enzymes and is located in the mitochondria of eukaryotic cells and in the cytosol of prokaryotes. In the conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, one molecule of NADH and one molecule of CO2 is formed.

The citric acid cycle is also called the Krebs cycle or the tricarboxylic acid cycle.

The net gain from one cycle is 3 NADH and 1 FADH2 as hydrogen (proton plus electron) carrying compounds and 1 high-energy GTP, which may subsequently be used to produce ATP. Thus, the total yield from 1 glucose molecule (2 pyruvate molecules) is 6 NADH, 2 FADH2, and 2 ATP.

In eukaryotes, oxidative phosphorylation occurs in the mitochondrial cristae. It comprises the electron transport chain that establishes a proton gradient (chemiosmotic potential) across the boundary of the inner membrane by oxidizing the NADH produced from the Krebs cycle.

cards.q: What is cellular respiration?
cards.a: Cellular respiration is the process of oxidizing biological fuels using an inorganic electron acceptor, such as oxygen, to drive production of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), which stores chemical energy in a biologically accessible form.
cards.cite: Cellular respiration is the process of oxidizing biological fuels using an inorganic electron acceptor, such as oxygen, to drive production of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), which stores chemical energy in a biologically accessible form.

cards.q: What are the main components of cellular respiration?
cards.a: Cellular respiration may be described as a set of metabolic reactions and processes that take place in the cells to transfer chemical energy from nutrients to ATP, with the flow of electrons to an electron acceptor, and then release waste products.
cards.cite: Cellular respiration may be described as a set of metabolic reactions and processes that take place in the cells to transfer chemical energy from nutrients to ATP, with the flow of electrons to an electron acceptor, and then release waste products.

cards.q: What is aerobic cellular respiration?
cards.a: If the electron acceptor is oxygen, the process is more specifically known as aerobic cellular respiration.
cards.cite: If the electron acceptor is oxygen, the process is more specifically known as aerobic cellular respiration.

cards.q: What is anaerobic cellular respiration?
cards.a: If the electron acceptor is a molecule other than oxygen, this is anaerobic cellular respiration.
cards.cite: If the electron acceptor is a molecule other than oxygen, this is anaerobic cellular respiration.

cards.q: How does anaerobic cellular respiration differ from fermentation?
cards.a: Anaerobic cellular respiration is not to be confused with fermentation, which is also an anaerobic process, but it is not respiration, as no external electron acceptor is involved.
cards.cite: Anaerobic cellular respiration is not to be confused with fermentation, which is also an anaerobic process, but it is not respiration, as no external electron acceptor is involved.

cards.q: What type of reactions are involved in cellular respiration?
cards.a: The reactions involved in respiration are catabolic reactions, which break large molecules into smaller ones, producing ATP.
cards.cite: The reactions involved in respiration are catabolic reactions, which break large molecules into smaller ones, producing ATP.

cards.q: What nutrients are commonly used in cellular respiration?
cards.a: Nutrients that are commonly used by animal and plant cells in respiration include sugar, amino acids and fatty acids, and the most common oxidizing agent is molecular oxygen (O2).
cards.cite: Nutrients that are commonly used by animal and plant cells in respiration include sugar, amino acids and fatty acids, and the most common oxidizing agent is molecular oxygen (O2).

cards.q: What is the maximum yield of ATP from one glucose molecule during cellular respiration?
cards.a: Biology textbooks often state that 38 ATP molecules can be made per oxidized glucose molecule during cellular respiration.
cards.cite: Biology textbooks often state that 38 ATP molecules can be made per oxidized glucose molecule during cellular respiration.

cards.q: Why is the maximum yield of ATP not reached?
cards.a: However, this maximum yield is never quite reached because of losses due to leaky membranes as well as the cost of moving pyruvate and ADP into the mitochondrial matrix, and current estimates range around 29 to 30 ATP per glucose.
cards.cite: However, this maximum yield is never quite reached because of losses due to leaky membranes as well as the cost of moving pyruvate and ADP into the mitochondrial matrix, and current estimates range around 29 to 30 ATP per glucose.

cards.q: How efficient is aerobic metabolism compared to anaerobic metabolism?
cards.a: Aerobic metabolism is up to 15 times more efficient than anaerobic metabolism (which yields 2 molecules of ATP per 1 molecule of glucose).
cards.cite: Aerobic metabolism is up to 15 times more efficient than anaerobic metabolism (which yields 2 molecules of ATP per 1 molecule of glucose).

cards.q: Where do post-glycolytic reactions take place in eukaryotic cells?
cards.a: The post-glycolytic reactions take place in the mitochondria in eukaryotic cells, and in the cytoplasm in prokaryotic cells.
cards.cite: The post-glycolytic reactions take place in the mitochondria in eukaryotic cells, and in the cytoplasm in prokaryotic cells.

cards.q: What is glycolysis?
cards.a: Glycolysis is a metabolic pathway that takes place in the cytosol of cells in all living organisms.
cards.cite: Glycolysis is a metabolic pathway that takes place in the cytosol of cells in all living organisms.

cards.q: What does glycolysis convert glucose into?
cards.a: The process converts one molecule of glucose into two molecules of pyruvate (pyruvic acid), generating energy in the form of two net molecules of ATP.
cards.cite: The process converts one molecule of glucose into two molecules of pyruvate (pyruvic acid), generating energy in the form of two net molecules of ATP.

cards.q: How many ATP molecules are produced during glycolysis?
cards.a: Four molecules of ATP per glucose are actually produced, but two are consumed as part of the preparatory phase.
cards.cite: Four molecules of ATP per glucose are actually produced, but two are consumed as part of the preparatory phase.

cards.q: What is the reaction equation for glycolysis?
cards.a: Glucose + 2 NAD+ + 2 Pi + 2 ADP → 2 pyruvate + 2 NADH + 2 ATP + 2 H+ + 2 H2O + energy
cards.cite: Glucose + 2 NAD+ + 2 Pi + 2 ADP → 2 pyruvate + 2 NADH + 2 ATP + 2 H+ + 2 H2O + energy

cards.q: What additional ATP is used in glycolysis?
cards.a: An additional ATP is used to phosphorylate fructose 6-phosphate into fructose 1,6-bisphosphate by the help of phosphofructokinase.
cards.cite: An additional ATP is used to phosphorylate fructose 6-phosphate into fructose 1,6-bisphosphate by the help of phosphofructokinase.

cards.q: What happens to pyruvate after glycolysis?
cards.a: Pyruvate is oxidized to acetyl-CoA and CO2 by the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC).
cards.cite: Pyruvate is oxidized to acetyl-CoA and CO2 by the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC).

cards.q: Where is the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex located?
cards.a: The PDC contains multiple copies of three enzymes and is located in the mitochondria of eukaryotic cells and in the cytosol of prokaryotes.
cards.cite: The PDC contains multiple copies of three enzymes and is located in the mitochondria of eukaryotic cells and in the cytosol of prokaryotes.

cards.q: What is produced during the conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA?
cards.a: In the conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, one molecule of NADH and one molecule of CO2 is formed.
cards.cite: In the conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, one molecule of NADH and one molecule of CO2 is formed.

cards.q: What is another name for the citric acid cycle?
cards.a: The citric acid cycle is also called the Krebs cycle or the tricarboxylic acid cycle.
cards.cite: The citric acid cycle is also called the Krebs cycle or the tricarboxylic acid cycle.

cards.q: What is the net gain from one cycle of the citric acid cycle?
cards.a: The net gain from one cycle is 3 NADH and 1 FADH2 as hydrogen (proton plus electron) carrying compounds and 1 high-energy GTP, which may subsequently be used to produce ATP.
cards.cite: The net gain from one cycle is 3 NADH and 1 FADH2 as hydrogen (proton plus electron) carrying compounds and 1 high-energy GTP, which may subsequently be used to produce ATP.

cards.q: What is the total yield from one glucose molecule in the citric acid cycle?
cards.a: Thus, the total yield from 1 glucose molecule (2 pyruvate molecules) is 6 NADH, 2 FADH2, and 2 ATP.
cards.cite: Thus, the total yield from 1 glucose molecule (2 pyruvate molecules) is 6 NADH, 2 FADH2, and 2 ATP.

cards.q: Where does oxidative phosphorylation occur in eukaryotes?
cards.a: In eukaryotes, oxidative phosphorylation occurs in the mitochondrial cristae.
cards.cite: In eukaryotes, oxidative phosphorylation occurs in the mitochondrial cristae.

cards.q: What does oxidative phosphorylation comprise?
cards.a: It comprises the electron transport chain that establishes a proton gradient (chemiosmotic potential) across the boundary of the inner membrane by oxidizing the NADH produced from the Krebs cycle.
cards.cite: It comprises the electron transport chain that establishes a proton gradient (chemiosmotic potential) across the boundary of the inner membrane by oxidizing the NADH produced from the Krebs cycle.

cards.q: What is the role of NADH in the Krebs cycle?
cards.a: The electron transport chain establishes a proton gradient (chemiosmotic potential) across the boundary of the inner membrane by oxidizing the NADH produced from the Krebs cycle.
cards.cite: The electron transport chain establishes a proton gradient (chemiosmotic potential) across the boundary of the inner membrane by oxidizing the NADH produced from the Krebs cycle.

cards.q: What is the primary function of cellular respiration?
cards.a: The primary function of cellular respiration is to transfer chemical energy from nutrients to ATP.
cards.cite: The primary function of cellular respiration is to transfer chemical energy from nutrients to ATP.

cards.q: What is the significance of ATP in cellular respiration?
cards.a: ATP stores chemical energy in a biologically accessible form.
cards.cite: ATP stores chemical energy in a biologically accessible form.

cards.q: What is the difference between aerobic and anaerobic respiration?
cards.a: Aerobic respiration uses oxygen as the electron acceptor, while anaerobic respiration uses a molecule other than oxygen.
cards.cite: Aerobic respiration uses oxygen as the electron acceptor, while anaerobic respiration uses a molecule other than oxygen.

cards.q: What is the role of phosphofructokinase in glycolysis?
cards.a: Phosphofructokinase helps to phosphorylate fructose 6-phosphate into fructose 1,6-bisphosphate.
cards.cite: Phosphofructokinase helps to phosphorylate fructose 6-phosphate into fructose 1,6-bisphosphate.

cards.q: What is produced during glycolysis besides ATP?
cards.a: Glycolysis also produces 2 NADH and 2 pyruvate molecules.
cards.cite: Glycolysis also produces 2 NADH and 2 pyruvate molecules.

cards.q: What is the fate of the high-energy GTP produced in the Krebs cycle?
cards.a: The high-energy GTP may subsequently be used to produce ATP.
cards.cite: The high-energy GTP may subsequently be used to produce ATP.

cards.q: What is the total number of ATP produced from one glucose molecule in glycolysis?
cards.a: The total number of ATP produced from one glucose molecule in glycolysis is 2 net ATP.
cards.cite: The total number of ATP produced from one glucose molecule in glycolysis is 2 net ATP.

cards.q: What is the significance of the proton gradient established during oxidative phosphorylation?
cards.a: The proton gradient (chemiosmotic potential) is essential for ATP production during oxidative phosphorylation.
cards.cite: The proton gradient (chemiosmotic potential) is essential for ATP production during oxidative phosphorylation.

cards.q: What is the role of the electron transport chain in cellular respiration?
cards.a: The electron transport chain oxidizes NADH and establishes a proton gradient across the inner membrane.
cards.cite: The electron transport chain oxidizes NADH and establishes a proton gradient across the inner membrane.

cards.q: What is the overall process of cellular respiration?
cards.a: The overall process of cellular respiration involves the oxidation of biological fuels to produce ATP and release waste products.
cards.cite: The overall process of cellular respiration involves the oxidation of biological fuels to produce ATP and release waste products.

cards.q: What is the relationship between glycolysis and the Krebs cycle?
cards.a: Glycolysis converts glucose into pyruvate, which is then used in the Krebs cycle after being converted to acetyl-CoA.
cards.cite: Glycolysis converts glucose into pyruvate, which is then used in the Krebs cycle after being converted to acetyl-CoA.

cards.q: What is the role of NAD+ in glycolysis?
cards.a: NAD+ is reduced to NADH during glycolysis, which is essential for energy production.
cards.cite: NAD+ is reduced to NADH during glycolysis, which is essential for energy production.

cards.q: What is the significance of the Krebs cycle in cellular respiration?
cards.a: The Krebs cycle is significant because it produces NADH and FADH2, which are crucial for the electron transport chain.
cards.cite: The Krebs cycle is significant because it produces NADH and FADH2, which are crucial for the electron transport chain.

cards.q: What is the main product of the Krebs cycle?
cards.a: The main products of the Krebs cycle include NADH, FADH2, and GTP.
cards.cite: The main products of the Krebs cycle include NADH, FADH2, and GTP.

cards.q: How many molecules of CO2 are produced from one glucose molecule in the Krebs cycle?
cards.a: From one glucose molecule, two molecules of CO2 are produced in the Krebs cycle.
cards.cite: From one glucose molecule, two molecules of CO2 are produced in the Krebs cycle.

cards.q: What is the role of the mitochondrial cristae in cellular respiration?
cards.a: The mitochondrial cristae are the site of oxidative phosphorylation in eukaryotic cells.
cards.cite: The mitochondrial cristae are the site of oxidative phosphorylation in eukaryotic cells.

cards.q: What is the significance of the electron acceptor in cellular respiration?
cards.a: The electron acceptor is crucial for the flow of electrons during cellular respiration, determining whether the process is aerobic or anaerobic.
cards.cite: The electron acceptor is crucial for the flow of electrons during cellular respiration, determining whether the process is aerobic or anaerobic.

cards.q: What is the net gain of ATP from one cycle of the Krebs cycle?
cards.a: The net gain from one cycle is 1 high-energy GTP, which may subsequently be used to produce ATP.
cards.cite: The net gain from one cycle is 1 high-energy GTP, which may subsequently be used to produce ATP.

cards.q: What is the role of oxygen in aerobic respiration?
cards.a: Oxygen acts as the final electron acceptor in aerobic respiration.
cards.cite: Oxygen acts as the final electron acceptor in aerobic respiration.

cards.q: What is the difference between the cytosol and mitochondria in terms of glycolysis and post-glycolytic reactions?
cards.a: Glycolysis occurs in the cytosol, while post-glycolytic reactions occur in the mitochondria in eukaryotic cells.
cards.cite: Glycolysis occurs in the cytosol, while post-glycolytic reactions occur in the mitochondria in eukaryotic cells.

cards.q: What is the process of cellular respiration?
cards.a: The process of oxidizing biological fuels using an inorganic electron acceptor, such as oxygen, to drive production of adenosine triphosphate (ATP).
cards.cite: Cellular respiration is the process of oxidizing biological fuels using an inorganic electron acceptor, such as oxygen, to drive production of adenosine triphosphate (ATP).

cards.q: What is the main purpose of cellular respiration?
cards.a: To transfer chemical energy from nutrients to ATP.
cards.cite: Cellular respiration may be described as a set of metabolic reactions and processes that take place in the cells to transfer chemical energy from nutrients to ATP.

cards.q: What are the waste products released during cellular respiration?
cards.a: Waste products are released after the flow of electrons to an electron acceptor.
cards.cite: with the flow of electrons to an electron acceptor, and then release waste products.

cards.q: What is the most common oxidizing agent in cellular respiration?
cards.a: Molecular oxygen (O2).
cards.cite: the most common oxidizing agent is molecular oxygen (O2).

cards.q: What is the yield of ATP from anaerobic metabolism?
cards.a: 2 molecules of ATP per 1 molecule of glucose.
cards.cite: anaerobic metabolism (which yields 2 molecules of ATP per 1 molecule of glucose).
